title: Matthew 3
subtitle: John the Baptist: Preparation and Proclamation; The Baptism of Jesus

sections.header: Verses 1–12 — John the Baptist: Preparation and Proclamation

heb.paragraph: The first word of John's preaching and later of Jesus' (4:17). Not merely 'feel sorry' but a fundamental reorientation — turning the whole direction of life. The noun form metanoia implies a transformation of understanding that produces changed behaviour.

Matthew's distinctive phrase (32 times), where Mark and Luke use 'kingdom of God.' The substitution reflects Jewish reverence for the divine name — 'heaven' stands for God. The kingdom is not a place but God's active reign breaking into human affairs.

hist.context: John the Baptist is simultaneously the last OT prophet and the first NT herald. His clothing (camel's hair, leather belt) deliberately echoes Elijah (2 Kgs 1:8). His message is pure OT prophetic tradition — repentance, fruit, judgment. But his message points forward to one who is qualitatively greater. The contrast between water baptism (v.11) and Spirit-and-fire baptism announces that something unprecedented is about to occur.

refs.note: A voice calling in the wilderness — the opening of the great consolation section of Isaiah. John's ministry inaugurates the new Exodus: prepare the way, the LORD is coming.
The messenger who prepares the way; the return of Elijah before the great day of the LORD. Matthew identifies John as this Elijah-figure explicitly in 11:14 and 17:12.
Elijah wore a garment of hair with a leather belt. John's clothing is a deliberate Elijah allusion, legible to any Jewish reader.

poi.name: Desert of Judea
poi.coords: Wilderness area between Jerusalem and the Dead Sea
poi.text: John preaches in the Desert of Judea — the traditional space of prophetic calling. Elijah lived in the wilderness; Isaiah's voice cries in the wilderness (40:3). The desert geography signals that John is in the prophetic tradition of those who speak before major covenant acts.

poi.name: Jordan River region
poi.text: People from Jerusalem, all Judea, and the whole Jordan region come out to John. The Jordan is Israel's boundary river — crossed by Joshua, associated with Elijah's departure. John at the Jordan is positioned at the covenant's geographical entry point into the land.

notes.note: Calvin: John fulfilled the role of preparatory herald with precision that made the Isaiah 40:3 connection unmistakable. The wilderness setting is theological as well as geographical — John stands outside the established religious structures as a prophetic voice calling Israel to the covenant faithfulness the institutions had abandoned.

Calvin on the two baptisms: John's water baptism is the sign; Christ's Spirit baptism is the substance. The outward washing points to the inward cleansing that only the Spirit effects. This is the consistent relationship between sacrament and Spirit: the sign accompanies and points to but does not produce the reality.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'Kingdom of heaven' explains Matthew's use of 'heaven' where Mark and Luke use 'God' — a Jewish circumlocution for the divine name. The note surveys what the kingdom announcement would have meant to a Jewish audience expecting eschatological restoration.
The NET note discusses the Trinitarian significance of the baptism: Father speaks, Son is baptised, Spirit descends — the three persons present at the inauguration of the Son's public ministry.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson notes that 'has come near' (engiken) is a perfect tense — the kingdom has arrived and its presence continues. This is not a future promise but a present announcement. John's repentance demand is the appropriate response to a present reality.

Robertson notes the baptism with Holy Spirit and fire is a single baptism with two effects. The winnowing fan (ptyon) is the familiar agricultural tool for eschatological separation — the coming one does what John announces but cannot accomplish.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Chrysostom: John began with the same preaching as Christ himself — repent, for the kingdom is at hand. He was the voice preparing for the Word, the lamp heralding the Sun. He preached repentance with greater urgency because the kingdom's arrival was imminent.

Augustine: He who baptizes with Holy Spirit and fire — the Spirit as purifying flame, the fire as consuming judgment. The same fire that refines gold destroys chaff. The dual meaning names the dual destiny that the kingdom's arrival creates.

sections.header: Verses 13–17 — The Baptism: The Trinity at the Jordan

heb.gloss: to be well pleased / delight in
heb.paragraph: The Father's declaration 'in whom I am well pleased' uses a verb expressing deep, settled delight — not mere approval but the Father's comprehensive satisfaction in the Son. The voice combines Psalm 2:7 (royal sonship) with Isaiah 42:1 (suffering servant). Jesus is both king and servant simultaneously.

The Spirit descends 'like a dove' (hōs peristeran). The comparison is to the manner of descent (gentle, visible) not the Spirit's nature. With the Father's voice and the Son in the water, Matthew presents the Trinity in a single scene — one of the clearest trinitarian moments in the Gospels.

hist.context: The baptism of Jesus is the most theologically concentrated event of Matthew 3. John's protest (v.14) acknowledges the anomaly: the one without sin submitting to a repentance-baptism. Jesus's answer — "to fulfil all righteousness" — points to his representative solidarity with humanity. The divine response is triadic: the Spirit descends, the Father speaks, the Son is designated. This is the first fully Trinitarian moment in Matthew, anticipating the baptism formula of 28:19.

refs.note: You are my Son, today I have become your Father — the royal coronation psalm. The Father's words at the baptism are a coronation declaration: Jesus is installed as the Davidic King.

Here is my servant whom I uphold, my chosen one in whom I delight — the first Servant Song. The Father's words also echo this: "with him I am well pleased." Jesus is simultaneously Davidic King and Isaianic Servant.
Take your only (agapētos) son... Isaac, whom you love. The language of the baptismal declaration echoes the Aqedah — the offering of the beloved son. The cross is already in view.

poi.name: Jordan River
poi.coords: Traditional crossing point; possibly near Jericho
poi.text: Jesus comes from Galilee to the Jordan to be baptized by John. The journey from Galilee south to the Jordan is roughly 100km. Jesus submits to a Judean prophet's rite, identifying with the people he has come to save — the geography of covenant solidarity.

tl.name: Malachi — last OT prophet; 400-year silence begins
John the Baptist born (6 months before Jesus)
John baptizes Jesus — this passage
tl.text: Jesus's baptism marks the beginning of his public ministry at approximately age 30 (Luke 3:23). The Trinitarian theophany — Father speaking, Spirit descending, Son standing — is the public commissioning. The 400-year prophetic silence ends with John; the Son's ministry begins at the Jordan.

notes.note: Calvin: Christ was not baptized for his own benefit but for ours. He submitted to the form appointed for sinners to show that he was taking our place. The sinless one enters the sinner's waters — this is the beginning of his substitutionary identification with those he came to save.

Calvin on the Trinitarian theophany: Here is a noble testimony to the undivided Trinity — the Son in the water, the Spirit as a dove, the Father in the voice. God solemnly presents his Son as entering on his prophetic office. Three persons distinct but not divided.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'to fulfil all righteousness' surveys the interpretive options: fulfilling the law's requirements, identifying with sinful humanity, inaugurating the new covenant. In Matthew, 'righteousness' typically refers to covenant-required conduct, not legal imputation.

The NET note on 'this is my beloved Son' identifies the allusion to Ps 2:7 and Isa 42:1 and discusses the text-critical issue: 'well pleased' (eudokēsa, aorist) may point to a specific moment of divine commitment — the Father's eternal pleasure in the Son expressed at this consecration.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson identifies 'to fulfil all righteousness' (plērosai pasan dikaiosynēn) as Jesus' self-identification with the covenant community he came to redeem. He enters the waters of repentance not because he needs repentance but because he is taking his place among those who do.

Robertson notes the schizō (torn open/split) vocabulary in Mark's parallel — heaven is not merely opened but torn apart. The heavenly affirmation echoes both Ps 2:7 (royal investiture) and Isa 42:1 (servant commissioning). The baptism is simultaneously royal inauguration and servant commissioning.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Chrysostom: Why was he baptized having no need of baptism? To fulfil the law, to sanctify the water, to give us an example, and to honour John. He who came to take away the sins of the world needed no washing — he came to sanctify the waters for us and to leave in them a power of sanctification.

Jerome: The Spirit descended not to enlighten him who needed no enlightenment but to show the world that the Spirit rests upon the meek and humble. The dove is the fitting symbol of the Spirit of peace.

ppl.name: John the Baptist
ppl.text: Inaugurates the new Exodus by preparing the way. The last voice of the OT prophetic tradition.

ppl.name: Jesus
ppl.text: Submits to baptism to fulfil all righteousness — solidarity with humanity, not confession of sin.

ppl.name: The Pharisees and Sadducees
ppl.text: Arrive at the Jordan seeking baptism without genuine repentance. John's rebuke anticipates Matthew 23.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased."NoteCombines Ps 2:7 (Son, kingship) and Isa 42:1 (beloved, Servant). Jesus is designated as simultaneously the Davidic King and the Suffering Servant — the two roles the gospel will show are one.

src.title: Jewish baptism
src.quote: Proselyte baptism (immersion for converts to Judaism) pre-dates John. John's baptism is distinctive: for Jews, as a sign of repentance, in anticipation of the coming one.
src.note: John's practice was unprecedented enough to give him his name: "the Baptizer."

src.title: Josephus on John
src.quote: Josephus describes John as a "good man" who "commanded the Jews to exercise virtue, both as to righteousness towards one another, and piety towards God" (Antiquities 18.5.2).
src.note: Josephus's account confirms John's historical existence and public prominence.

rec.text: The baptism of Jesus as the pattern for Christian baptism: the rite of entry into the new covenant community. The Trinitarian formula of 28:19 derives from the Trinitarian event of 3:16–17.
The dove descending on Jesus has been the primary symbol of the Holy Spirit in Christian art from the early church through the Renaissance.

rows.label: Repentance Bearing Fruit: The Baptist's Demand
rows.text: Produce fruit in keeping with repentance. John refuses the Pharisees' ancestral identity as grounds for exemption from judgment. Covenant membership is demonstrated in character, not claimed through lineage.

rows.label: Repentance Bearing Fruit: The Baptist's Demand
rows.text: Produce fruit in keeping with repentance. John refuses the Pharisees' ancestral identity as grounds for exemption from judgment. Covenant membership is demonstrated in character, not claimed through lineage.

rows.label: The Trinitarian Baptism — 3:13-17
rows.text: Father, Son, and Spirit all present at the Jordan. The Son submits to the sinner's baptism; the Spirit descends; the Father speaks — royal inauguration and servant commissioning simultaneously.

tx.title: 'Will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire'
tx.content: Matthew uses 'and fire' (kai pyri); some argue this is a single baptism with dual effect, others two separate baptisms.
tx.note: The single-baptism reading fits the grammar and the prophetic background (Ezek 36:25-27; Mal 3:2-3). The fire purifies the repentant and judges the impenitent.

positions.name: John's baptism was preparatory; Christian baptism is distinct
positions.argument: John baptized in water for repentance; Christian baptism is in the name of the Trinity and linked to the Spirit's work (Acts 2:38; 19:1-6).

positions.name: Continuity of covenant sign
positions.argument: Both point to cleansing and covenant membership; the difference is degree of fulfilment, not essential nature.

themes.note: Matthew 3 establishes three foundational realities: the kingdom has arrived and demands repentance; the sinless Son identifies with sinners from his ministry's first moment; and the triune God publicly consecrates the Son for his redemptive work.

coaching.tip: 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is near' (v. 2). John's message anticipates Jesus' (4:17). Repentance isn't feeling sorry—it's turning around. The kingdom's proximity demands immediate response.

'This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased' (v. 17). The Father's voice combines Psalm 2:7 (royal sonship) with Isaiah 42:1 (servant). Jesus is both king and suffering servant—categories Israel kept separate.

prayer_prompt: As the voice from heaven declares 'This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased,' consider asking God whether you live from the security of His delight in you — or whether you are still trying to earn it.
